video_title: Fertilizer Shock: Why Farmers Are Getting Crushed First
transcript: I have heard of a single place in the world who has gone without fertilizer. But the whole situation like this great famine that's coming because nobody could get fertilizer is completely false. You're seeing a lot of double-digit percentage changes on all the fertilizer. I think this is something we're going to be struggling through for a very long time. >> Hello and welcome to Wealthion. I'm Maggie Lake. Joining me today to discuss the outlook for the global fertilizer market and what it means for food supplies is Josh Linville, vice president of fertilizer at StoneX Financial. Hi Josh, great to have you on with us. >> Hey, thanks for having me on. >> Uh we have an active community that is very interested in real assets, but I have to say even among this group, I think fertilizer falls into the category of something that those of us outside farming sort of took for granted, but of course that's completely changed now with the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. So I thought it'd be good to maybe start with a little bit of a reality check. Uh and I know there's a lot going on, you'll bring us up to date, but we've seen very alarmist headlines as is the trend the trend these days. Are the disruptions we've seen so far severe enough that we're facing a global food shortage? >> I think the short answer is no. And a little bit more of a clarification is that let's say it's your point. If somebody had brought the term fertilizer before all of this had started up with 13, 14 weeks ago with the Strait of Hormuz, most people would look at you just wondering what you're talking about. It wasn't even something they knew. Um Washington D.C. very much in that mix, you talked to a lot of politicians, they don't wouldn't know what you were talking about. Well, all of a sudden it's popular and it's the big story and it's across all the news. And how do you get a a following? How do you get popular? Well, it's not the sunshine and rainbow type stories. It's the you know, people want tune in the news for the car crashes and the famines and things like that. So you saw a lot of new covers step in the marketplace and talk about this global famine. So far I can tell you with everything that's happened, it's not just the straight, it's the Chinese export programs, it's lack of European production, it's a lot of things around the world. 13 and 1/2 weeks later, I haven't heard of a single place in the world who has gone without fertilizer. Now, has demand been lower? Could that have an impact on some yields later on this year? Absolutely, and that's something we're still watching for. But the whole situation like this great famine that's coming because nobody could get fertilizer is completely false. Everybody that we talked to, North America included, got everything that they've needed. >> That's amazing, and it's really good to dispel that because, I mean, you're right, you know, for somebody who worked in news for years, if it bleeds, it leads. So we all try to we try to push against the tendency to do that. Um and it's rife on the internet, so glad we clarified that. So there are there's no food crisis, but but this is a these are major disruptions, and and we just it just feels like supply chains everywhere we're rethinking what resiliency looks like. So based on what's happened, um what are we seeing? Where is the impact being felt? Is it in prices? >> Well, it it's in the fertilizer prices. And really, this is something that's being digested more on the farm side. Because when you look at things like corn and beans and wheat, and you look at where the price was the day before the straight shut down, and you look at where those prices are today, there's not a massive differential. Uh I try to get into the pattern where every Friday on Twitter, I do a price comparison of all the major fertilizers today versus what it was at the start of the deal. And I also look at like corn and beans and wheat to put that into perspective. And you're seeing a lot of double-digit percentage changes on all the fertilizer. Grains have done very little. So that's the unfortunate part of it. Now, are grocery stores moving the price up? Yes, but I think it's just a natural trend of those pricing. It's the farmer, in my sense, that's really bearing the weight of everything going on around the world. >> Which we know must ultimately have implications. So, how how bad is it? What are you hearing from farmers? What's the situation on the ground for them? >> I think it depends on the product. When you look at things like phosphate and potash, these are two products that you don't necessarily have to have every single year. If the price is too high like it is this year, you can see demand lower because the farmer either decides to skip that application rate or they reduce the application. That can have limited impact on the yield if they've been, you know, applying a lot of it in the years leading up to this. They can reduce that application with very little impact on the overall yield. Nitrogen's a little bit different, right? You have to have the nitrogen to raise the yield. There's supposed to be a direct correlation there. This year, I think it's going to be challenged. I think based on a lot of the things we've already seen, which we've not finalized this fertilizer year, but we're getting close. It's starting to look like farmers are probably saying, "Listen, if I usually put down 250 counts of nitrogen, I might scale it back to 240, 235. There shouldn't be that big of an impact." And people might think, "Well, Josh, that's a small percentage. Why would I even care?" You think about that small percentage over the global nitrogen market, that turns out a very big number of tons that are no longer being digested. And so, you take that demand destruction, you take some of these markets kind of spreading out the demand, you know, maybe not buying quite as early as they normally would, and I think that's what's really helped this market from getting into a supply shortage. >> So, interesting. It's very similar to what we've been talking about in other areas of materials or resources is that, you know, with the strait closed, people have relied on inventories or they they've sort of they've made their way around it, but I know there's concern that that's only temporary band-aid, right? And so, the longer it stays open, the more there are concerns that we're going to we're going to sort of reach the end of the road in terms of our, you know, ability to find workarounds. Um do you have any kind of idea what the lag on that is? Like when would we get to a point with the closure of the street, and we'll broaden it out cuz I know it's not the only choke point, but does has anybody been able to come up with a scenario that we say if it is still closed by this point, then we might have more serious problems? >> I would say no. And again, we have never seen anything like this. And this is what makes it so difficult. We're all making a bunch of educated guesses on it, and we're all not that educated when it comes to this type of thing. Who would have ever thought that we'd see so many major supply points having so many problems all at the same time? And so we can sit there and we can theorize and say, "Oh, if it happens in another couple weeks, another couple months, it's going to have this impact, that impact." We really do not know. Um I don't I'm sitting there even stopping myself from making a guess because that's all it's going to be is just a guess on what happens. But I mean, obviously, the longer it goes on, the more tons we lose. Um Take for example just the urea market. When you look at Iran, Oman, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia, these are three of the world's biggest urea exporters in the world. They combine for over 13 and 1/2 million tons per year. And the tons from there have been next to nothing for three plus months now. I can't tell you what the drop-dead date is where if we hit June 15th, that's just where there's no coming back from it. But I can tell you I think that this is going to have impacts on pricing and supply at least in the spring of '27. I think this is something we're going to be struggling through for a very long time. >> Yeah, and I think that's the worry from people who really closely track these markets as you do because I've heard it echoed in other parts of the commodity market that it's sort of given investors a sense of complacency or false sense of security that, "Oh, the these economies are resilient. They find workarounds. There are workarounds." When actually it's it's sort of borrowing from the future, you know, using these cushions until they're And then you don't have that anymore. And And there's a feeling like that's the things are going to grind to a halt. We're going to hit that, you know, where the Wile E. Coyote off the cliff and it's suddenly going to We're going to feel some sort of drop. And I don't think it's alarmist to say that. I mean, these are real assets that move through real supply chains. So, when you talk about something unprecedented that's never happened before and these major producers offline, you know it's going to show up somewhere, right? And And but I guess the hope is that things are repaired while the workarounds still still operate and and we hobble by. That's the best case scenario, I suppose. >> Yeah, yeah. One of the biggest things that we have right now, we were able to get through this period that we did. And frankly, this could not have happened at a worse time of year in the lead up to the northern hemisphere spring season. The thing the market has going for it across all the products is we're moving into the summer months. And globally speaking, that is a very, very slow demand time frame. So, we can kind of heal a little bit. We can try and sit there and be a little more patient. Prices, we have seen some prices coming off. Like on the nitrogen side, we're seeing some bearishness there. The problem is when you do start to think long-term and again, this is going back to the educated guess. You [clears throat] start to think about it in terms of negotiation power. And that's really how a price is determined, right? If you're the seller and I'm the buyer, I'm trying to get lower, you're trying to get higher. But if you feel more pressure to sell to me than I do to buy from you, the price is coming down. I I I control more of that. As we look forward into the remainder of 2026 and into the start of 2027, the supplies that were missing, that are not there anymore, hurts the global S&D. And so, who does that put more pressure on? It's the buyer. Cuz that manufacturer for the rest of this year and into the next year is going to be able to sit there and say, "Supplies are tight. You need the tons. I can afford to sit back and wait for you to step forward and buy my price cuz I just don't feel that pressure. I feel like I'm in a more powerful position." And that's where you start to get some of these points of view that as a whole these markets are going to be higher priced. It doesn't mean that prices cannot you know dip and rise and do all this sort of stuff. But overall the overall price should be higher than what we would consider normal. >> So we've been so focused on the stray rightly so because of it's been in the headlines and again as we say unprecedented and you know there were as only a couple months ago military conflict hot military conflict. You've been watching China closely and sort of indicating this is actually an under appreciated risk and we should be paying more attention to China's role in the fertilizer market. What's happening there? What's changing? >> So it's important to note a lot of this will look at China and try to sit there and figure out what they're going to do based on free market thinking because that's where our capitalist that we're come from US and the places like that. China's communist they're going to look at it differently. And before we even got to 2026 the Chinese government came out and said we are going to restrict the exports of phosphate and urea until August. And what they're doing is and this is my educated point of view on all this but a communist nation's biggest danger is not an invasion by something like the US. It's not anything like that. Their biggest danger is an uprising by an angry population. By blocking exports you do two things. You keep domestic supply solid and you keep domestic prices very very low versus the rest of the year. That keeps your farmer base happy and guess what? Farmer base accounts for about 25% of their population. So by doing this you're keeping a lot of your people really really happy. You're you're being seen as hey you're guiding this thing in the right direction. Well done government. I like you. I don't want to see anything different. But that's a big problem for the rest of the world. Historically speaking China is the biggest phosphate exporter and it's not even a close second. And they have exported next to nothing all of 2026 and then fortunately I don't even know if they're going to start exporting in August. They may delay it. Urea, we hadn't seen much of anything, but we're starting to get rumors they may start exporting a little bit sooner because their production has been good, supplies are sitting good. That may be a great influx of supply into the marketplace that help prices fall a little bit further than what they already are. >> So, a little bit of relief coming on on the urea front. It what you described happening in China, why wouldn't other countries start doing that? Are we moving into an an environment or is there a risk we're moving into an environment with these strains that could last years where people say, "Let me get my Let me make sure domestically I'm set before I thought what I'm doing in terms of export?" >> Yeah, we're already starting to see protectionism starting to pop up out there. Uh the Egyptian government came out and said, "Hey, any urea exports from here on out are subject to a duty rate. We're going to make it harder for those tons to go out. We are doing our own people a favor by doing this." Uh you've seen the Australian government starting to step in and they're only working with certain parties, which is kind of odd that they're not widespread across the entire nation, but they are working with a couple companies to say, "Bring the vessels of urea in because we're afraid there won't be enough because the importers don't want to take that risk and we'll guarantee the downside price protection on this deal." You're starting to see a little bit more of a surge of US people sitting there calling for, "Hey, if you're going to restrict some of these imports, we need to do the same thing on the exports." When times get tough, when prices get high and everybody's struggling, that protectionism starts to take hold. That's the short-term piece of it. I've continued to hope what we're going to see is a resurgence in new production because ultimately, that's what fixes this thing long-term. That's what makes it so that when this happens, we've got extra supply capacity. We don't need to worry about it nearly as much. Our problem is we've gotten complacent on depending on all these other countries to supply our fertilizer needs. And I think what you're going to start to see is some of these countries say, "We're not going to do that anymore. We're going to help support more of our own production. Yeah, maybe it can't quite compete with the imports, but we're at least going to have that backstop. >> That that that's again so interesting and and echoes across what we've seen seen many other parts of the commodity space. It's not the supply itself. It's the production refining shipping. It's getting it to the end user that's the choke point, not the actual supply itself. >> Right. >> [clears throat] >> And it's not just you know farm commodities, right? You're starting to see it in like computer chips and precious metals and things like that. And a free market works fantastically as long as everybody is operating on a free market point of view. But the second that protectionism starts to come in, it starts to for lack of a better term bastardize the entire process and you start to see a lot more hiccups. You start to see a lot more struggles and that's when it starts to break down. Free market people don't like because it's cold, it's calculated. It doesn't care about your feelings. It does not care if you survive or not. It cares about balancing its S&D every single minute of every single day. And the second you start to intervene it can no longer do that. >> Well, when you're talking about export controls, when you're talking about supporting the bottom of prices, um government indirect government intervention in prices, this is a very different environment. Does it feel like this is temporary temporary or is there a chance this becomes more structural in nature? >> I'm hoping it's I'm hoping it's short-term and the reason I say that is I still hope that if we're going to go through all this pain, if the farmer's going to struggle, if the market's going to struggle like it is I'm hoping that we will learn from it and we will build from it. What I mean by that is building more production, building more capacity so that the S&D globally speaking is looser. We don't have to rely on the imports. We can we can solve some of these things. We have the capacity to do it. But we've got to get the government behind us. We have to get some of that support to get these new production lines up and running. And so far the attention has been focused by the Trump administration. It's been great. Do not get me wrong. I always have to kind of backpedal and be like, "Listen, I'm not trying to be overly, you know, stifle on what they've done." They've put a lot of attention, a lot of effort into trying to help fix the fertilizer market. It's just been a little not guided in the right direction. They're focusing on products that we really don't need help with and they're passing over the ones we really do need help with. >> What are the ones we do need help with? >> Urea is a great one. Um when you look across it and hydrous we produce most of what we need. Urea we produce a pretty good chunk of what we need, but urea we import over 5 million tons per year. Half of those flows come from the Middle East. A quarter of it comes from Russia. So, that's where you start to look step back and look at and say, "Urea is really what we need to start increasing our production rates, start to get less reliant on those regions, especially given what's happening around the world." Phosphate we're self-sufficient, but the problem is without a new phosphate rock reserve found, we're going to continue to lose production. That's why you're starting to see those production rates fall and fall and fall. The rock quality is getting lower. It's getting harder to work with and they're running out of it. So, unfortunately without the phosphate rock reserves, you need to start partnering up with other countries like Norway who found a massive phosphate rock reserve, with Morocco who we're currently blocking with countervailing duty rates, with Saudi Arabia who unfortunately is blocked behind the Strait of Hormuz. Longer term thinking, we've got to figure out how to work with these countries closer to get long-term supply agreements if we can't fix it ourselves, that is. >> Yeah. It It who would be Do we have um and again, um I'm imagining what we're all thinking because you've been covering this market really closely. Most of us have been, you know, chugging along without having to think about it too much. Do we have companies here in the US that would be in a position to ramp up domestic production? Is there uh the the sort of sector capacity to do that or or do we have to really start from ground zero? >> We can. Um and frankly speaking, the best chance that we actually increase production is with the companies that are already in the space. Let's take nitrogen production. If you and I were to get together and say we want to build a brand new world scale for nitrogen production facility, we've got to find funding for three, four, five billion US dollars. We're probably going to struggle with that. But even if you do, well, now you've got to get all the government red tape cut and everything through to actually start producing it. But when it comes down to it, the base product, that LNG, that gas input is plentiful and it's cheap. Everything in the world from a free market point of view says we ought to be increasing our production because the economics are there. But for some of the companies already in the space, why would they want to do that? They like it as it is. Their input cost is very low. Their finished good cost is very, very high because of all the global situation and tight supplies. And frankly, do they want to take the risk themselves? So, they're not expanding. What I'd like to see is some sort of a program that helps a new company build a new production facilities. It builds supply and it builds competition. Both are massive wins for farmers. >> Uh. >> But we're just not seeing that yet. >> that's interesting. You need a whole new player in it. Who are the major players in that space? >> When you look at it from a nitrogen standpoint, CF and Koch are the two that really stand out. Um, they are the two biggest ones out there. Nutrien is course is a big player as well. >> So interesting. Um, and and it sort of speaks to the challenges of that reindustrialization, that reshoring of these supply chains, um, and why people say you can't flip a switch, right? It's It's It's a lot more complicated. And so you're talking about heavy capital investment, timelines that don't sort of match up with the shortages we're seeing. So, it may not be structural, but it certainly sounds like a multi-year problem to clear this, especially the longer it goes on. Is that Is that correct? >> Yeah, absolutely. And that's why we continue to sit there and look at this and say if we finally have the attention that this this market needs. We finally have the base where we can kind of stand on and start building this into the future. Again, if we have to go through the pain, let's fix it. I'd rather go through the pain and fix it today than pass that on to the next generation of farmers, the next generation of this industry that's coming. >> Do we have the talent to do this? Do we have the the sort of I mean, who who's been, you know, everyone's running to Silicon Valley and to all these other companies. Do we have the actual know-how if somebody wanted to start a new company? Like who's the fertilizer entrepreneur out there? >> Yeah, there's and that's a problem. I think a lot of people, if they are looking to get into space, they're looking at other technologies. You're seeing a lot of attention be paid to like green and blue energy produce nitrogens. And that's the exciting thing and that's what everybody's focused on. The problem with that, yes, it produces nitrogen, but it doesn't sit stand on its own two feet without massive subsidies. It's a very high cost production thing. We don't see anybody really going back to the old-style production because again, that's old, it's dirty in their mindset, things like that. And again, yeah, to the point, if you and I have three, four, five billion dollars to invest, are we really wanting to put that in fertilizer so where [clears throat] it's going to take years before that plant even produces its first ton? And then what [clears throat] does the market even look like by that point? It is a massively risky bet. And that money can be put in a lot safer, quote-unquote, sexier investments out there. >> Yeah, this is where this is where governments as the mitigating risk factor come into play, right? We see this a lot in emerging markets, which is where I've had these conversations, but sometimes you need the government's presence not to control the market, but to stand there as a third party mitigating, being a bridge between the private sector and the the funding and these companies who are trying to do it. It's wild to think that if you hang around the UN, you see a lot of this. Um, these multilateral organizations or or also play that role, but it hasn't been something talked about domestically, but I have a feeling we're all going to have to sort of get up the learning curve on that. >> Yeah, my big my big thing has always been I I don't want the government to be a part of it. I'm a big Reagan fan. I'm from the government and I'm here to help are some of the scariest words in the world. But it does need to act as a buffer. It needs to keep those barriers in when the market gets too far one side, it needs to sit there and slap it on its hand and say, "Nope, get back to the center. That's where you belong." And that's where I think we are. >> Right. Which is Which is, by the way, there are some There are some organizations who sit on that risk in in the middle on risk and and are not really involved, but they're just the the sort of they'll take on the risk that no one else wants to as a backstop, almost like insurance, I guess. Um you mentioned Russia before. I want to mention I want to bring up Ukraine. For all of another So, China's one big thing not enough people are talking about in your mind. What about Ukraine? Because again, all this attention on the stray, but if I remember back from when Russia first invaded Ukraine, there were a lot of concerns about fertilizer there as well or in that region and the disruptions. What's happening there? Well, so Ukra- Ukraine themselves are not major fertilizer exporters, but they did play a major help in lay Russian anhydrous tons export to the rest of the world. Russia would produce the tons within their own territory and they'd ship it through a pipeline that ran through the east and south Ukraine out to the Odessa region and they'd load down the vessels and they'd sail to the rest of the world. They'd sell 4 and 1/2 million tons per year this way. It's a major route. Turns out when you attack a country, they don't let you put a lot of tons in that pipeline. It's effectively shut out and it's been a major loss from a nitrogen standpoint globally speaking. We've lost the world's biggest anhydrous exporter. The fortunate thing is we really thought late '21 '22 when Russia initially invaded Ukraine, we thought we were going to lose Russia as a whole. I mean, Starbucks was pulling out, McDonald's was pulling out. There was no way anybody was going to do fertilizer business. But that wasn't the case. They continued export urea and UAN and phosphate and potash. And they are one of the largest of each one of those major products. So, fortunately, while some of the world like the uh Europe, Canada, Australia refuse to do business, a lot of those products can still find other homes around the world. If we lose Russia, that's about the only thing that could make this bad situation worse than what it is today. >> Yeah, which I hate to even say those here you say those words because it just when we think it can't get any worse, it seems to. So, couple of last questions. It's amazing amazing to to to sort of get such a deep dive with all your knowledge on this. Um is there is there an investment play on this? Like normally when we see commodities move and especially again I mentioned we have a a community of folks who invest in real assets or interested in real assets. Um we would see tight supplies, prices go higher. That's that's an investment opportunity. Is it the same in fertilizer? >> It should be, but again we're not seeing nearly as much because I think when a lot of people look at it, they look at the fertilizer and say, "Yeah, margins are good today. What is it in a year, 2 years, 3 years, 5 years?" And if I've got that kind of money to invest, I really want to get into that market. Now, maybe they are looking at and saying, "I want to invest in places like CF and Nutrien, but you know, Mosaic's." But I don't really want to get into the sector because at the end of the day, there's a lot better place I can put that money. There's a lot better returns that are guaranteed long terms than what fertilizer is. It's just to build that out of nothing is a Herculean effort. >> Yeah, which I think um you know, answers the question from an investment point of view, but only serves to underscore the concern about the future of these supply chains and whether we can get So, our right now farmers are bearing the brunt. Ground zero seems to be like on the back of farmers and agriculture. Is it inevitable that they will pass along those prices if this does not get rectified? Will we ultimately see higher food prices? >> It's a possibility if we start to find out later this year. Let's say the crop tours start up. Usually people if they start coming back and say, "Oh, the yields are bad or whatever." The market will somewhat ignore that and say, "Yeah, but we'll see what happens with harvest." I think this year if they start coming back and saying, "Hey, we've done the crop tour. These yields are not looking good." I think they are going to be taken a little more seriously because the fear is going to be there that they didn't fertilize, so the yield's going to be impacted and that's going to be the first thing the market gets to see to actually show there's going to be lower yields across the board. But, if the grain markets don't reflect it, if you don't see corn, bean, wheat, cotton, everything out there, if those prices don't rise, then it really shouldn't impact anything further down the line because you haven't changed anything beyond what the farmer is selling. >> Mhm. And it's always it's always about the the cross section. So, let's let's watch that and the weather and then we'll have another conversation about ags. Maybe that's going to be the investment play. Josh, so appreciate your time. This is really important topic and really uh interesting and necessary to get the actual facts about what's happening on a granular level and not just like the internet 2-minute experts who are warning of of of widespread famine. I think I'm going to sleep a little bit better tonight. So, I appreciate it. Thank you for that. >> Well, just if you see me throw out some outlander thing like we're all going to starve to death, just know I'm trying to get another 10,000 followers on Twitter. That's all it [laughter] is. >> Or that it's real. If I see it from you, I might think it's real. Appreciate your time today, Josh. >> Yeah, thanks for having me on.
